title: Abstract
content: TiO 2 nanotubes (TNTs) photocatalyst was synthesized by hydrothermal reaction, while the heterostructure CdS(or Bi 2 S 3 )TiO 2 nanotube photocatalysts were prepared by direct precipitation reaction.The photocatalysts were studied by TEM, EDX, XRD and XPS to characterize its structure and chemical compostion.Their photocatalytic activities for reducing CO 2 to CH 3 OH under visible light irradiation and the performance for CO 2 adsorption were also investigated.The results indicate that the synthetical TNTs photocatalyst is almost in good and uniform shapes, with the diameter of 17.77 nm and the surface area of 99.57m 2 g.The amounts adsorbed of CO 2 on TNTs are 0.269 mmolg at 1 atm pressure and 298 K,which are larger than those on other two heterostructure photocatalysts.The adsorption isotherms of CO 2 on three photocatalysts can be well described by the Freundlich adsorption equation.The modification of Bi 2 S 3 or CdS can enhance the visible light absorbance and photocatalytic performance of TNTs photocatalyst.The photocatalytic activity, the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 and visible light absorption capability of Bi 2 S 3 -modified TNTs photocatalyst are all better than those of CdS-modified TNTs photocatalyst.Especially, the largest yield of methanol on TNTs photocatalyst modified by Bi 2 S 3 under visible light irradiation over 5 h is 224.6 molg, which was about 2.2 times as large as that of TNTs.

title: Experimental
content: The synthesis of TNTs was performed according to the method reported by Kasuga et al. 25.In a typical preparation process, TNTs were preparaed with 2 g TiO 2 (rutile) and 80 mL NaOH aqueous solution (10 molL) in a hydrothermal autoclave at 110 C for 24 h, and then cooled down to room temperature.The precipitate were filtered off, and subsequently washed with distilled water until pH 7.The prepared TiO 2 was mixed with 0.1 molL HCl aqueous solution and dispersed for 3 min in a supersonic field, then washed with distilled water until pH 7, and finally dried at 400 C for 4 h. The preparation of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 was carried out as follows: First, the TNTs (1.00 g) were dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and dispersed for 30 min.Secondly, 0.09 g Bi(NO 3 ) 3 5H 2 O was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water and was added to the above solution under the strong stirring.Thirdly, 0.1 g Na 2 S was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water and was added to the above sample slowly.Finally, the suspension was dispersed for 30 min in a supersonic field and then filtered, washed with distilled water and ethanol 3 times, respectively, and dried at 240 C for 4 h. The preparation of TNTs-CdS photocatalysts was similar to that of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 .Firstly, the TNTs (1.00 g) was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and dispersed for 30 min in a supersonic field.Secondly, 0.37 g Cd(Ac) 2 2H 2 O was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water and was added to the above solution under the strong stirring.Thirdly, 0.11 g Na 2 S was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water and was added to the ethanol suspension slowly.Finally, the suspension was dispersed for 30 min in a supersonic field and then filtered, washed with distilled water and ethanol 3 times, respectively, and dried at 400 C for 4 h. Nitrogen adsorption measurements were carried out at 77 K by using an accelerated surface area and porosimeter system (ASAP 2010, Micromeritics) equipped with commercial software of calculation and analysis for determination of the textural properties of all thephotocatalysts studied 26,27.The UV-vis spectroscopy in the 200-800 nm was measured with a Daojin UV-2550PC Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy.The XRD patterns were obtained at room temperature using a MSAL-XD2 diffractometer with Cu K radiation (operated at 36 kV and 30 mA, 0.15406 nm).The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive Xray (EDX) spectrum were performed on a JEM-2100HR (200 kV, Japan) operated at 120 kV.The XPS were collected by a Kratos AXis Ultra (DLD) with pass energy of 20 KeV, and the excitation of the spectra was performed by means of monochromatized Mg K radiation.Correction of the energy shifts due to static charging of the samples was accomplished by referencing to the C1s line from the residual pump-line oil contamination taken at 284.6 eV.The CO 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 298 K were obtained separately on a RUBOTHERM magnetic suspension balance 28.The temperature of both adsorption and desorption can be adjusted and maintained constant by an internal temperature sensor.Meanwhile, the pressure can be changed stepwise through the gas flow rate. The photocatalytic reaction was performed in a continuous-flow reactor system as shown in Fig. 1.A 500 W Xe lamp located in the quartz cool trap was the irradiation source and the UV light below 400 nm was removed by a 2.0 M sodium nitrite solution 29.As shown in Fig. 2, the relative energy of the lamp was less than that of sunlight.Prior to experiments, sodium hydroxide (0.80 g) and absolute sodium sulfite (2.52 g) were dissolved in 200 mL distilled water.This solution was then put into a photochemical reaction instrument.Before irradiation, ultrapure CO 2 was bubbled through the solution in the reactor for at least 30 min to ensure that all dissolved oxygen was eliminated, then, 200 mg of catalyst powder was added into above solution, and the irradiation lamp was turned on to start the photoreaction.Ultrapure CO 2 was continuously bubbled through the above solution in the reactor during the whole irradiation.The radiation time was 5 h.A needle-type probe was inserted into the solution in the reactor to withdraw a small liquid sample at 1 h, 2 h, 2.5 h, 3 h, 3.5 h, 4 h, 4.5 h and 5 h, respectively.The concentration of methanol in the samples was analyzed using a GC9560 gas chromatograph equipped with a flame ionization detector and a stainless steel packed column (Porapak-Q, 2 mm 3 m).The blank test was performed in the dark with the catalyst and CO 2 under the same experimental conditions.The methanol has not been detected.

title: Results and discussion
content: Fig. 3 presents the adsorption-desorption isotherms of N 2 on the TNTs, TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 .It can be seen from Fig. 3 that all tested photocatalysts give the type IV isotherm according to the BET classification 30.Each isotherm shows a distinct hysteresis loop, which is associated with capillary condensation taking place in mesopores.It means that all the photocatalysts are mainly mesoporous.Fig. 4 shows the DFT pore size distributions for the photocatalysts.It indicates that the photocatalysts have mainly mesopores.Table 1 lists the structure parameters of the photocatalysts.The data in Table 1 indicated that the BET surface area and total pore volume of the TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 became somewhat smaller, but the mean pore diameter became larger compared to the TNTs.In other words, the surface area of the photocatalysts followed the order: TNTs TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 TNTs-CdS, however, the average pore diameter followed the contrary order: TNTs TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 TNTs-CdS.Thus, the difference may be attributed to the formation of CdS or Bi 2 S 3 on the surface of TNTs, which probably resulted in the complete filling of the small pores, and enlarging of the average pore diameter 29,31. The UV-vis absorption spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 5. From Fig. 5, it can be seen that TNTs can well respond to ultraviolet light of wavelength shorter than 400 nm.The band gap (E g ) of the TNTs is also estimated to be 3.12 eV from the plot of the (ah ) 12 versus photon energy (h ) as shown in Fig. 5 29, which is very close to the value of reported rutile (3.0-3.1 eV) 32.In contrast to TNTs, TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 and TNTs-CdS has a strong adsorption in the visible region.The upward shift of light absorbance for TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 and TNTs-CdS in the wavelength region from 400 to 800 nm can be explained in terms of the light absorption of yellow CdS and dark grey Bi 2 S 3 dispersed on the TNTs.Therefore, the light yellow TNTs-CdS and dark grey TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 have better absorption capability for visible light than the white TNTs.In addition, it can be also seen from Fig. 5 that the redshift in the absorption spectra of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 is larger than that of TNTs-CdS, which may be because that the band gap of Bi 2 S 3 is smaller than that of CdS 33.All results indicate that the visible light absorption capability of TNTs can be greatly enhanced by the mofification of Bi 2 S 3 as well as CdS and that the visible light response performance of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 is better than that of TNTs-CdS. The XRD patterns of different photocatalysts are given in Fig. 6.As shown in Fig. 6 The detailed microscopic structure of the photocatalysts was further investigated by TEM and HRTEM.Fig. 7 shows the TEM image of TNTs.It can be observed from Fig. 7 that the TNTs are transparent in nature and almost in good and uniform shapes, about 300 nm in length, and approximately 10-20 nm in diameter, which is close to the mean pore diameter (17.77 nm) by nitrogen adsorption.Fig. 8 In order to further investigate the chemical compostion and oxidation state of CdS and Bi 2 S 3 on the TNTS, XPS measurements were performed.Fig. 10 shows the higher resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p and Cd 3d core level for TNTs-CdS, and Bi4f and Ti2p core level for TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 , respectively.As shown in Fig. 10(a), the Cd 3d core level XPS spectrum has two sharp peaks at 404.9 eV (Cd 3d52) and 411.6 eV (Cd 3d32), which are in good agreement with the previously reported values 34,35 and can be assigned to Cd 2 of CdS nanoparticles.This confirms that the CdS nanoparticles are successfully deposited into the TNTs.Meanwhile, it also can be noticed from Fig. 10(b) that the two strong peaks at about 158.7 eV and 164.3 eV are respectively attributed to the Bi4f 72 and Bi4f 52 binding energy 363738, indicating that they are Bi 2 S 3 particles on the surface of TNTs.In addition, it can be noticed from Fig. 10(c) and (d) that there are two obvious peaks located at 458.3 eV (or 458.5 eV) and 463.8 eV(or 464.3 eV), which correspond well to Ti2p 32 and Ti2p 12 39,40, respectively.The spin-orbit separation of 5.5 (or 5.8) eV can be assigned to the presence of Ti 4 in the TiO 2 -CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 heteroarchitectures 41.Table 2 gives the XPS fitting data for TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 .It can be seen from Table 2 that the mass concentrations of the element Cd and Bi are 12.60 and 12.32, respectively, from which the mass concentrations of CdS and Bi 2 S 3 nanoparticles on the surface of TNTs can be calculated are 16.1 and 14.2, respectively.It is apparent that the mass concentration of CdS is significantly larger than that of Bi 2 S 3 on the surface of TNTs Table 3. FWHM: the full width at a half of the maximum height of the peaks; BE: the binding energy; RSF: relative sensitivity factor.3.6.Isotherms of CO 2 on the photocatalysts Fig. 11 shows the isotherms of CO 2 on the photocatalysts at 298 K.It can be seen that the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 on the TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 and TNTs-CdS have lower adsorption capacities of CO 2 compared to the TNTS, and the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 on TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 are higher than those of TNTs-CdS.It meant that after surface modification, the adsorption capacities of the modified TNTs for CO 2 were decreased.It is also worthy noticing that the order of the adsorption capacity of these photocatalysts for CO 2 is nearly in agreement with the order of the surface area of these samples.In other words, the larger the surface area of sample, the higher its adsorption capacity for CO 2 is.Thus, it was inferred that the surface area of these samples played a key role in the enhancement of CO 2 adsorption.Therefore, in order to understand the effect of the surface area of each sample on its adsorption performance for CO 2 , the Langmuir and Freundlich equation was used to fit the isotherms.The Langmuir 28 and Freundlich 42 model can be expressed respectively as follows: where q is the amount adsorbed in equilibrium with the concentration of adsorbate in gas phase (mgg), q max is the maximum adsorption amount (mgg), p is the equilibrium pressure of the adsorbate in gas phase (bar), and K is the equilibrium constant of adsorption, 1n is the heterogeneity factor and n is a measure of the deviation from linearity of adsorption.Table 3 shows the parameters of the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm equations.It can be seen from Table 3 that r 2 values of the Freundlich isotherm equation are larger than 0.993.It was indicated that the Freundlich isotherm model fits better than the Langmuir model and that the adsorption behaviours of CO 2 on all three photocatalysts can be well described by the Freundlich adsorption equation.It is also found that the values of the parameter n are higher than unity, indicating that adsorption of CO 2 onto photocatalysts is a favorable physical process 42.Therefore, the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst for CO 2 increases with the increase of its surface area. In addition, the adsorption capacity (0.269 mmolg) of TNTs for CO 2 at 1 atm pressure and 298 K is much lower than those of MOFs (2.5 mmolg) 43, ZIF-8 (7 mmolg) 44, zeolite13 (1.77 mmolg) 45 and activated carbon (2.92 mmolg) 28, which may be due to the smaller surface area of TNTs compared to those high surface area adsorbents. The methanol yields of the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 12.The largest yields of methanol on P25, TNTs, TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 photocatalysts under visible light irradiation for 5 h were 91, 102.5, 159.5 and 224.6 molL, respectively.The results clearly indicate that the photocatalytic performance of TNTs can be greatly improved via incorporating inorganic semiconductor nanoparticles into the TNTs though the surface area and the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 of TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 is smaller than those of TNTs.On the one hand, the absorption spectrum of TNTs can be sensitized with Bi 2 S 3 and CdS nanoparticles, thus the visible light absorption capability of TNTs can be greatly enhanced by the mofification of Bi 2 S 3 and CdS, which will significantly improve the photocatalytic activities of TNTs due to the introduce of sensitizers.On the other hand, when CdS or Bi 2 S 3 is loaded onto the surface of TNTs, the heterojunction structure may form, which not only improves the separation of electrons and holds, prevents charge-carrier recombination and but prolongs the lifetime of photo-carriers in photocatalysts 464748.As a result, the photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts are also greatly enhanced by heterogeneous compound.In addition, it also can be seen from Fig. 12 that the photocatalytic performance of TNTs is slightly larger than that of the commercial titania powder P25.A possible reason is that the surface area of TNTs is much larger than that of P25 (52.7 3.6 m 2 g),which has been measured independently by a number of investigators 49. For comparison with the methanol yields of CdS and Bi 2 S 3 33, the average production rate of methanol of all photocatalysts was summarized in Fig. 13.As shown in Fig. 13, the average production rate of methanol followed the order: Bi 2 S 3 TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 CdS TNTs-CdS TNTs P25.It is clear that the average production rate of methanol of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 or TNTs-CdS is respectively smaller than that of Bi 2 S 3 or CdS.However, it is also found that the photocatalytic activity of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 is better than that of TNTs-CdS.This is because the responsing performance towards visible light, the surface area and the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 of Bi 2 S 3 are all better than those of CdS. The mechanism of photocatalytic reaction can be discussed with use of energy-band theory based on the relative position of the potentials of conduction band, valence band and oxidation-reduction.Fig. 14 shows the mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to CH 3 OH.It can be observed from Fig. 14 that the potentials of conduction band of Bi 2 S 3 and CdS 5051525354 are more negative than all those of six-electron reduction of CO 2 (g), H 2 CO 3 , CO 3 2-, etc. to methanol in water.Therefore, whether the state of CO 2 in water is a form of H 2 CO 3 or CO 3 2-, it can be reduced to methanol by one of the three photocatalysts.

title: Conclusions
content: The TNTs, with tube length of 200 nm and a pore diameter of 17.77 nm, was synthesized by hydrothermal reaction and acid washing, while the modified TiO 2 nanotubes photocatalysts were prepared by direct precipitation reaction.The experimental results indicate that Bi 2 S 3 and CdS modification can decrease the surface area and the amounts adsorbed of CO 2 , but greatly increase the visible light absorption capability and the photocatalytic activity of TNTs.The adsorption behaviours of CO 2 on all modified TNTs can be well described by the Freundlich adsorption equation.The largest yields of methanol on TNTs, TNTs-CdS and TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 photocatalysts under visible light irradiation for 5 h were 102.5,159.5 and 224.6 molL, respectively.The mechanisms of photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to CH 3 OH can be explained by the energy band theory. Fig. 1.Schematic of experimental setup for photoreduction of CO2 with H2O. Fig. 2. Relative energy spectrum of sunlight and Xe lamp used in this work. Fig. 4. DFT pore size distributions for the photocatalysts. Fig. 5. UV-vis absorption spectra of the photocatalysts. Fig. 6.XRD patterns of different photocatalysts. , five characteristic peaks of TNTs are observed at 2A values of 27.43 , 36.0 , 41.24 , 54.32 , 56.63 and 69.00 , which can be indexed as the tetragonal rutile phase.The XRD patterns are in very good accord with that of the reported JCPDS database card (JCPDS file: 75-1757), indicating that the TNTs is the pure rutile nanotubes without any anatase titania.At the same time, it can also be seen from Fig. 6 that the XRD patterns of TNTs-CdS heterostructures have other three peaks at 2A values of 26.46 , 43.89 and 51.98 besides all characteristic peaks of TNTs, which correspond to (1 1 1), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) of cubic CdS, repectively, according to JCPDS file: 89-0440.These results clearly indicate that cubic CdS has been prepared in the heterostructures of TNTs-CdS successfully.In addition, there are three other peaks at 2A values of 24.93 , 28.6 and 52.61 in the pattern of TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 excluding the characteristic peak of rutile titania, which are in good agreement with the standard peaks of orthorhomibc Bi 2 S 3 (JCPDS file: 17-0320).The results indicate that Bi 2 S 3 in TNTs-Bi 2 S 3 heterostructure is orthorhomibc Bi 2 S 3 nanocrystals. Fig. 7. TEM image of TNTs. (a) and (b) shows the TEM images of TNTs-CdS heterostructures.As shown in Fig. 8(a), it clearly indicates that the cubic CdS nanoparticles and TNTs aggregates are clearly observed.It is clearly observed from Fig. 8(b) that CdS nanoparticles have been deposited on the internal or external surface of the TNTs.Fig. 8(c) is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the TNTs-CdS heteroarchitectures.From Fig. 8(c), it is also noticeable that the lattice fringes of CdS and TNTs in the crystal system are observed.The composition of the nanoparticles is determined by an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) experiment, which is carried out in the TEM.The EDX spectrum of the TNTs-CdS is shown in Fig. 8(d), the characteristic peaks are associated with Ti, O, S and Cd, where Ti and O peaks are from TNTs, Cd and S peaks come Fig. 8. (a) and (b) TEM images of the TNTs-CdS; (c) HRTEM image of the TNTs-CdS heterostructures; and (d) the corresponding EDX spectrum. Fig. 9. (a) and (b) TEM images of the TNTs-Bi2S3; (c) HRTEM image of the TNTs-Bi2S3 heterostructures; and (d) the corresponding EDX spectrum. Fig. 10.Higher resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d for TNTs-CdS (a), Bi4f for TNTs-Bi2S3 (b), Ti2p for TNTs-CdS (c) and Ti2p for TNTs-Bi2S3 (d). Fig. 11.Isotherms of CO2 on the photocatalysts. Fig. 12.The methanol yields of the photocatalysts. Fig. 13.The average production rate of methanol of all photocatalysts: (a) P25; (b)TNTs; (c)TNTs-CdS; (d) TNTs-Bi2S3; (e) CdS; (f) Bi2S3. Fig. 14.The mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with H2O to CH3OH. Porous structure parameters of the photocatalysts. XPS fitting data for Cd 3d of TNTs-CdS and Bi 4f of TNTs-Bi2S3. Parameters obtained by fitting of Langmuir and Freundlich equation.
